coredata.dc:title: A new approach in functionalization of carbon nanoparticles for optoelectronically relevant carbon dots and beyond
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Xianyan
Liang, Weixiong
Wang, Ping
Bunker, Christopher E.
Coleman, Montrez
Teisl, Lindsay Rose
Cao, Li
Sun, Ya-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDots), generally small carbon nanoparticles with surface passivation by a soft corona-like layer of mostly organic species, have been actively pursued for potential applications in optoelectronics, including various functions that have been served by some popular fullerene derivatives. For the preparation of CDots, chemical functionalization of pre-processed and selected small carbon nanoparticles by organic molecules, so far mostly molecules with primary and secondary amine groups, has been an effective method. In this study, carbon nanoparticles were functionalized by N-ethylcarbazole (NEC) under microwave-assisted reaction conditions for NEC-CDots, analogous but with advantages to the CDots of surface functionalization by poly(N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK, which holds a special place in optoelectronics). NEC molecules can apparently be activated under the reaction conditions for reactive functionalities such as radicals to bind to surface carbons of the nanoparticles, consistent with the observed high stability of NEC-CDots. These dots, likely with a unique surface passivation scheme, exhibited optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics similar to those found in other high-performance CDots. The potentially broad applicability of the new functionalization approach is discussed, so are implications of the unique surface passivation of carbon nanoparticles by the carbazole moieties.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from Air Force Office of Scientific Research through the program of Dr. Kenneth Caster, NASA/EPSCoR , Air Force Research Laboratory , and the South Carolina Space Grant Consortium is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A cartoon illustration on the structure of CDots in general, with more specifically the carbon nanoparticle surface binding and passivation by the carbazole species. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Visible absorption spectra of the NEC-CDots in THF solution (——), the aqueous suspended small carbon nanoparticles (− − −), and THF solution of the sample from the control experiment for the same processing of blank NEC without carbon nanoparticles (−∙∙ −). The inset: (upper) X-ray powder diffraction pattern of the small carbon nanoparticles; and (lower) photos for the aqueous suspension of the small carbon nanoparticles (A, with somewhat more scattering) and the THF solution of NEC-CDots (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
An AFM image of the NEC-CDots on mica surface, with height analysis results of the selected dots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The proton NMR spectrum of the NEC-CDots in DMSO‑d 6 (lower) is compared with that of NEC molecules (upper). In the former, the crossed peaks are associated with DMSO and water in the NMR solvent, and the circled sharp peaks are due to residual THF and hexanes from the cleaning of the dot sample (the peak integrations are relatively not so significant). The integrations for CH 3:CH 2:ring-H are 3:1.2:6.1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The FT-IR spectrum of the NEC-CDots is compared with that of NEC molecules, both of which were measured with the use of the accessory for solid samples on the instrument. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorption and fluorescence spectra (from left to right corresponding to 400 nm, 440 nm, 480 nm, 520 nm, 560 nm, and 600 nm excitations) of the NEC-CDots in THF solution, and the excitation wavelength dependence of observed fluorescence quantum yields (○). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The variations (deviations from the average represented by the solid line) in the observed fluorescence quantum yields of NEC-CDots at 400 nm (○) and 440 nm (Δ) excitations with respect to the dot sample being treated by refluxing in toluene for up to 25 h. The dashed lines define the range of +5% and −5% deviations from the average. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Stern-Volmer quenching plots for fluorescence intensities (440 nm excitation) of NEC-CDots in DMF solution at different DNT (□) and DEA (○) concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A new approach in functionalization of carbon nanoparticles for optoelectronically relevant carbon dots and beyond

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Emerging Materials and Technology, Clemson University, Clemson, SC, 29634, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Emerging Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Air Force Research Laboratory, Propulsion Directorate, Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Ohio, 45433, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical and Materials Engineering Department, University of Dayton, Dayton, OH, 45469, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDots), generally small carbon nanoparticles with surface passivation by a soft corona-like layer of mostly organic species, have been actively pursued for potential applications in optoelectronics, including various functions that have been served by some popular fullerene derivatives. For the preparation of CDots, chemical functionalization of pre-processed and selected small carbon nanoparticles by organic molecules, so far mostly molecules with primary and secondary amine groups, has been an effective method. In this study, carbon nanoparticles were functionalized by N-ethylcarbazole (NEC) under microwave-assisted reaction conditions for NEC-CDots, analogous but with advantages to the CDots of surface functionalization by poly(N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK, which holds a special place in optoelectronics). NEC molecules can apparently be activated under the reaction conditions for reactive functionalities such as radicals to bind to surface carbons of the nanoparticles, consistent with the observed high stability of NEC-CDots. These dots, likely with a unique surface passivation scheme, exhibited optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics similar to those found in other high-performance CDots. The potentially broad applicability of the new functionalization approach is discussed, so are implications of the unique surface passivation of carbon nanoparticles by the carbazole moieties.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDots) [1,2], generally characterized by a nanoscale structural configuration of small carbon nanoparticles with various surface passivation schemes (Fig. 1 ), have attracted much attention, as reflected by the large and ever increasing number of publications in the literature [3–11]. Among the known characteristic properties of CDots are their bright and colorful fluorescence emissions in the visible spectrum, extending into the near-IR. A mechanistic framework for the fluorescence properties [7,8,12], as supported by results from experimental investigations in various aspects [12–16], is such that upon the photoexcitation of the core carbon nanoparticles in CDots (with the surface passivation layer illustrated in Fig. 1 optically transparent at the excitation wavelengths), there must be rapid charge separations in the nanoparticles. The resulting electrons and holes from the charge separations must be trapped at the abundant surface defect sites of the nanoparticles. Emissive excited states could be formed by radiative recombinations of the trapped electrons and holes, conceptually similar to what is known in conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). Also similar to semiconductor QDs, rather interestingly, is the need for surface protection/passivation (such as the capping of CdSe nanoparticles by ZnS for the famous CdSe/ZnS QDs) [17,18] to achieve high fluorescence performance. The various surface passivation schemes for the small carbon nanoparticles in CDots (Fig. 1) have improved fluorescence quantum yields from the 1–2% at best generally found for “naked” carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous dispersion [19,20] to 70% or higher in some CDots of effective surface passivation [21,22]. The necessity of surface passivation and its major effects on the fluorescence performance of CDots have been demonstrated more vividly in a recent study on the surface functionalization and then defunctionalization of carbon nanoparticles, which resulted in a substantial increase and then a dramatic decrease, respectively, in the observed fluorescence brightness [23]. In fact, chemical functionalization of preexisting small carbon nanoparticles (harvested from various pure carbon powders) with organic molecules or species has been an effective method, commonly referred to as the deliberate chemical functionalization synthesis, for the preparation of high-performance CDots [21–26].

Most of the reported chemical functionalization syntheses for CDots have been based on amidation reactions of various amino molecules with the carboxylic acid moieties on the carbon nanoparticle surface, where the surface-bound carboxylic acids are typically generated in the oxidative acid treatment as a key step in the processing and selection of the carbon nanoparticles [20]. These nanoparticles harvested by the processing and selection procedures for the functionalization reactions are generally without any defined crystal structures and with surface populations of various combinations or arrangements of sp2 and sp3 carbons. In a more recent study [27], it was shown that the small carbon nanoparticles were similar to fullerenes in terms of participating in thermally induced copolymerization reactions with vinyl molecules like N-vinylcarbazole. The polymerization reactions were rationalized as being radical propagation and addition in nature [27], namely that the carbon nanoparticles, just like fullerenes [28–30], could be functionalized by radical species. Equally significant in the comparison with fullerenes is that the photoexcited carbon nanoparticles are excellent electron acceptors, again similar to fullerenes [31], yet at the same time they are extraordinarily potent electron donors as well [13,32]. In fact, the photoinduced redox characteristics of CDots have promoted the increasingly popular and extensive investigations on their uses, for the purpose of replacing the expensive fullerene derivatives in many cases, in various optoelectronic devices [6,7,33–37]. Poly(N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) as a well established hole transport polymer holds a special place in optoelectronics [35,38–40], which makes the coupling of PVK with CDots particularly interesting, and potentially valuable to optoelectronic applications. Similarly interesting and valuable is the introduction of carbazole and other optoelectronically relevant molecules and species into CDots without invoking polymerization for added or different purposes, such as structurally and composition-wise mimicking simple fullerene derivatives like 1-(3-(methoxycarbonyl)propyl)-1-phenyl[6.6]C61 (PCBM) that have found extensive uses in optoelectronics [41–43].

The ability for carbon nanoparticles to participate in radical reactions, as made evident by the copolymerization with vinyl molecules discussed above, can and should be exploited for new approaches in the deliberate chemical functionalization synthesis of CDots by using different carbon nanoparticle surface functionalization schemes from the commonly used amide linkages. In the study reported here, we functionalized the small carbon nanoparticles with N-ethylcarbazole (NEC, Fig. 1), a molecule structurally similar to N-vinylcarbazole except for the absence of the vinyl group, thus the same coupling of carbazole with CDots without polymerization or any polymers in the resulting dot sample. The apparently successful functionalization of the carbon nanoparticles, as reflected by the effective surface passivation effects for the observed high fluorescence performance of the CDots, may be rationalized in terms of binding of the NEC-derived reactive species, such as radicals generated in the microwave irradiation, to surface carbons of the nanoparticles (Fig. 1). Also interesting and significant is the previously unknown potent surface passivation of the carbon nanoparticles due to their interactions with the planar carbazole species. These interactions are topologically and probably also mechanistically analogous to those associated with the π stacking in surface modification of other nanoscale carbon allotropes, such as the widely studied noncovalent functionalization of carbon nanotubes by planar aromatic species [44–47], as highlighted in the next section. Similarly highlighted and discussed are the general applicability of the new chemical functionalization approach for introducing other desired surface species or functionalities into CDots, including those more valuable to optoelectronics, and the related different surface passivation schemes for high-performance CDots of specific properties or functions.

The carbon nanopowder sample (US1074) was acquired from US Research Nanomaterials, Inc. N-Ethylcarbazole (NEC) was obtained from Beantown Chemical, N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) from Avocado, and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) from TCI. Toluene was purchased from Burdick & Jackson, tetrahydrofuran (THF) from Acros Organics, hexanes from Avantor, and nitric acid from Fisher Scientific. Deuterated solvents for NMR measurements were supplied by Cambridge Isotope Laboratories.

UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV2501-PC spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were acquired on a Jobin-Yvon emission spectrometer equipped with a 450 W xenon source, Gemini-180 excitation and Triax-550 emission monochromators, and a photon-counting detector (Hamamatsu R928P PMT at 950 V). 9,10-Bis(phenylethynyl)-anthracene in cyclohexane was used as a standard in the determination of fluorescence quantum yields by the relative method (matching the absorbance at the excitation wavelength between the sample and standard solutions and comparing their corresponding integrated total fluorescence intensities). FT-IR spectra were measured on a Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer with the Single Reflection ATR accessory for solid samples. NMR measurements were performed on a Bruker Advance 500 NMR spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging experiments were carried out by using the acoustic AC mode on a Molecular Imaging PicoPlus AFM system equipped with a multipurpose scanner and a NanoWorld Pointprobe NCH sensor. The height profile analysis was assisted by using the SjPIP software distributed by Image Metrology.

Small carbon nanoparticles were harvested from the commercially acquired carbon nanopowder sample in procedures similar to those reported previously [27]. In a typical experiment, the carbon nanopowder sample (4 g) was refluxed in concentrated nitric acid (8 M, 200 mL) for 48 h. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and allowed to settle for 12 h. The supernatant was collected, and concentrated via evaporation to remove nitric acid as the more volatile component in the supernatant. Water was added, followed by evaporation. The procedure was repeated several times until pH about 1 in the resulting solution. The solution was dialyzed in a membrane tubing (molecular weight cut-off ∼500) against fresh water for 48 h for the small carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous dispersion. Upon evaporation to remove water, a solid-state sample of the nanoparticles was obtained.

The small carbon nanoparticles (70 mg) were ground with NEC (500 mg) for well mixing, and to the mixture was added THF (5 mL), followed by sonication in a bath sonicator (VWR 250D) for about 1 h. The solvent THF was removed via purging with nitrogen gas to obtain a solid-state mixture of the small carbon nanoparticles dispersed in NEC in a scintillation vial. Separately, silicon carbide powders (150 g) in a silica crucible casting dish were prepared as a solid-state bath, and heated in a conventional microwave oven at 500 W for 3 min. The vial with the reactant mixture was immersed in the pre-heated silicon carbide bath for microwave treatment at 700 W for 5 min. Post treatment, the sample vial was taken out of the oven. Back at ambient temperature, THF (3 mL) was added to the sample, and then sonicated for 30 min, followed by the removal of THF via purging with nitrogen gas to obtain a solid-state mixture. As a repeat of the same procedure, the mixture was treated with microwave irradiation and then post-treatment processing under the same conditions. After four more repeats (thus a total of six treatment – post-treatment processing cycles), the reaction mixture was cleaned by washing with hexanes in three repeats, including the use of sonication for 30 min in each, and then dissolved in THF (5 mL). For further cleaning, the THF solution was precipitated into hexanes (45 mL). The cleaned sample was dispersed in a THF-hexanes mixture (70/30 v/v) with sonication for 30 min. The resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 1400 g for 15 min to collect the dark-colored supernatant as a solution of the NEC-CDots. The average yield in terms of the amount of starting carbon nanoparticles in the final CDots was around 40%.

Carbon nanoparticles processed in oxidative acid treatment could be dispersed in water due to the introduction of oxygen-containing moieties on the particle surface in the treatment, and the aqueous dispersion could be fractionated gravimetrically for the harvesting of smaller carbon nanoparticles as precursor in the preparation of CDots. Experimentally, commercially supplied pure carbon soot containing mostly nanoparticles was refluxed in concentrated nitric acid, followed by a work-up procedure already reported in the literature [27] to obtain the targeted small carbon nanoparticles in a relatively stable suspension in water (Fig. 2 ). The stability of the suspension against precipitation under ambient conditions allowed a more quantitative determination of optical absorptions by the small carbon nanoparticles in the visible spectrum (Fig. 2), where there should be no contributions from n-π* transitions associated with oxygen containing species on the nanoparticle surface. These nanoparticles of sizes averaging around 5 nm in diameter according to electron microscopy imaging results [27] are largely amorphous, generally lack of any defined diffraction peaks in the X-ray powder diffraction analysis (Fig. 2). For the small sizes of the nanoparticles and their processing history, there must be a significant population of defect sites on the nanoparticle surface, and as such that the passivation of the surface defects in various functionalization or interaction schemes represents the essence of CDots (Fig. 1).

N-Ethylcarbazole (NEC, Fig. 1) was used to functionalize the small carbon nanoparticles under microwave-assisted thermal reaction conditions. Experimentally, a solid-state mixture of the pre-processed and selected small carbon nanoparticles with NEC was prepared. Among the specifically targeted characteristics of the mixture was the well dispersion of the carbon nanoparticles in NEC, such that individual nanoparticles were surrounded by NEC molecules in the mixture. The mixture was treated with microwave irradiation in a conventional microwave oven, for which a consideration was that the irradiation would target more selectively the carbon nanoparticles for their larger cross-sections toward the microwave irradiation, thus higher local temperatures and more reactions on the nanoparticle surface. The treatment was in multiple cycles, each including the microwave treatment and then the re-mixing/dispersion of the sample before the next cycle. The purpose for the approach of multiple treatment cycles was to ensure an even heating across the entire sample macroscopically in the thermal processing (despite the expected preferential absorptions of microwave irradiation by the nanoparticles and the associated local heating during the irradiation, as discussed above). Post-treatment, the reaction mixture was cleaned by mostly anti-solvent precipitations to obtain NEC-functionalized carbon nanoparticles, denoted as NEC-CDots (Fig. 1), whose visible absorption spectrum is largely the same as that of the precursor small carbon nanoparticles in a stable suspension (Fig. 2). This is expected because NEC does not absorb in the visible, and also on the basis of similar comparisons for other CDots in previously reported studies, with a general conclusion that the surface functionalization of carbon nanoparticles by various organic species does not fundamentally alter visible absorptions of the nanoparticles [8]. In a control experiment, a blank NEC sample without any carbon nanoparticles was similarly treated with microwave irradiation, followed by the same cleaning procedure. The rather small amount of products thus obtained exhibited only very weak or negligible visible absorptions (Fig. 2).

As highlighted in the introduction section, molecules structurally similar to NEC but containing a vinyl group, such as N-vinylcarbazole, could be copolymerized with the small carbon nanoparticles in thermally induced radical polymerization reactions [27]. Without a vinyl group NEC is obviously incapable of any classical radical polymerization reactions. Instead, in the microwave treatment there might be the creation of NEC-derived reactive species, such as radicals from losing an α-hydrogen on the ethyl group in NEC (Fig. 1) [48–50], that could bind to surface carbons of the carbon nanoparticles. The localized heating around the carbon nanoparticles due to their preferential absorption of microwave irradiation might have facilitated and enhanced the binding reactions. However, the amount of radicals, if they are indeed the intermediates, is unlikely substantial and sufficient to result in meaningful decomposition of the nanoscale carbon, as found in other radical reactions under very different reaction conditions [51]. Regardless of the possible mechanistic details, the outcome of the microwave treatment was the functionalization of the small carbon nanoparticles by NEC. The resulting nanoscale configuration adheres to the general definition on CDots (Fig. 1) [8,12], thus the designation as NEC-CDots. The dot sample is soluble in solvents like THF and toluene for colored solutions that appear rather similar to the corresponding nanoparticle suspension, consistent with the comparison of their visible absorption spectra (Fig. 2).

The NEC-CDots on mica surface were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM). In a representative image shown in Fig. 3 , individual dot sizes estimated by the image height analysis are mostly in the range of 4–10 nm in diameter, consistent with expected slight size increases associated with the functionalization of the starting carbon nanoparticles (on the order of 5 nm in diameter [27]) by NEC molecules.

The solubility of NEC-CDots in common organic solvents made it possible for more quantitative solution-phase NMR characterization of the dot sample in solution. Shown in Fig. 4 are 1H NMR spectra of the NEC-CDots and for comparison free NEC molecules in DMSO‑d 6. The signals of the protons in the former, while all being present, are considerably broader than those in the latter, largely as expected for the much reduced mobility of the NEC species in the dot sample due to their association with the carbon nanoparticles. Similarly affected by the association are the signal integrations, CH 3:CH 2:ring-H's of about 3:1.2:6.1 in the NEC-CDots versus 3:2:8 in free NEC molecules, suggesting that the functionalization of carbon nanoparticles affects the CH 2 the most, then the ring-H's, and the CH 3 the least. It may thus be argued that the N-CH2- and the carbazole aromatic plane both play roles in the functionalization (covalent and noncovalent) and the associated surface passivation effects in NEC-CDots.

FT-IR spectra of the NEC-CDots and NEC molecules are compared in Fig. 5 . The spectral features are generally broader in the former than those in the latter. In addition to their shared carbazole peaks around 1230 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, and 1600 cm−1 and aliphatic carbon-hydrogen vibrations in the 2850–3000 cm−1 region, the extra absorptions around 1720 cm−1 may be assigned to carbonyl moieties in the NEC-CDots, as commonly observed in FT-IR spectra of other CDots.

The results from sample characterizations are all consistent with the functionalization of the small carbon nanoparticles by NEC molecules, deserving the designation as NEC-CDots. The primary mode of the functionalization is likely covalent in nature, with an educated guess for a bond between a nanoparticle surface carbon and the α-carbon in the ethyl group of NEC (Fig. 1). In such a structure, the planar carbazole heteroaromatic moiety would be lying flat on the carbon nanoparticle surface (Fig. 1), topologically similar to the arrangement for π stacking in the non-covalent functionalization of carbon nanotubes [44–47]. Therefore, strong interactions of the heteroaromatic carbazole with the carbon nanoparticle surface should provide effective surface passivation effects in the NEC-CDots for high fluorescence performance. Indeed, the observed fluorescence emission properties of NEC-CDots are consistent with such an expectation.

Similar to other CDots from deliberate chemical functionalization syntheses, such as the EDA-CDots [EDA for 2,2’-(ethylenedioxy)bis-(ethylamine)] that have been used as a benchmark [32], the NEC-CDots are brightly fluorescent in the green spectral region corresponding to 400–450 nm excitations (Fig. 6 ), with observed quantum yields up to 20% or higher. The fluorescence emissions are excitation wavelength dependent in the rather characteristic pattern that is generally shared by CDots with a variety of surface moieties for the required passivation and from different syntheses [1,52,53], such that the emission band progressively shifts to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength moves toward the red (Fig. 6). Equally characteristic is the excitation wavelength dependence of fluorescence quantum yield, which tracks the gradual decrease in absorption (Fig. 6), again a feature generally shared by other CDots [52,53]. Mechanistic implications of these rather characteristic patterns have been explored in the literature [8], with a rationale based on available experimental results that these characteristic spectroscopic behaviors are reflective of the unique excited state properties and processes of the core carbon nanoparticles in CDots. For the definition of CDots as small carbon nanoparticles with various surface passivation schemes, it is therefore no surprise that these behaviors are shared by CDots in general, including those prepared by synthesis methods other than the deliberate chemical functionalization. One such method is the thermal carbonization processing of carbon-rich organic precursors, with the processing protocol and conditions satisfying the requirement that the core carbon nanoparticles in the resulting dot samples must be well defined [53].

The NEC functionalization in NEC-CDots is very stable with respect to the associated surface passivation effects for the fluorescence emission properties. Experimentally, the NEC-CDots were refluxed in toluene for up to 25 h, which resulted in only marginal changes/decreases in observed fluorescence quantum yields (Fig. 7 ). The high stability is consistent with the assumed formation of covalent carbon-carbon linkages as the primary mode of functionalization in the microwave-assisted reactions of carbon nanoparticles with NEC, as discussed above.

CDots are also characterized by their unique photoinduced redox properties as both electron acceptors and donors [13], which have been exploited for various photon-electron conversion applications [7,14], including those in optoelectronic devices [7,33–37]. A convenient method for the evaluation of the redox properties has been the fluorescence quenching due to charge transfers with electron rich or deficient molecules as quenchers [13,32]. In this study, 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) were used as electron acceptor and donor quenchers, respectively. A Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of NEC-CDots by DNT is shown in Fig. 8 . The quenching is apparently very efficient, reflected by the significant upward deviation due to static quenching even at still low DNT concentrations, and by the large Stern-Volmer constant (K SV) of 63 M−1 obtained from the linear portion of the plot for the very low DNT concentrations only (Fig. 8). The results suggest that these dots in the photoexcited states are extraordinarily strong electron donors. With average fluorescence lifetimes (τ F) on the order of 5–10 ns found in most CDots [27,32,54], the K SV value corresponds to the quenching rate constant (k q = K SV/τ F) of larger than 6 × 109 M−1s−1, beyond the diffusion control. Such a phenomenon of “faster than diffusion” often observed in fluorescence quenching studies of CDots has been rationalized as being due to an average quenching radius extending beyond the dot edge due to extraordinarily strong and efficient electron transfers [32]. This is often accompanied by significant upward curvatures in the Stern-Volmer plots, namely more classical static quenching contributions [55], as also found here in the fluorescence quenching of NEC-CDots by DNT (Fig. 8).

CDots in photoexcited states are both electron acceptors and donors, though generally they are stronger donors and than acceptors. In the case of NEC-CDots here, results from the fluorescence quenching by DEA suggest that they are abnormally weak acceptors. The Stern-Volmer plot also shown in Fig. 8 for the electron donor DEA as quencher yields a K SV value of 1.5 M-1), more than an order of magnitude smaller than that for the quenching by DNT discussed above. It is also much smaller than the value of ∼18 M−1 previously reported for the fluorescence quenching of the EDA-CDots as a benchmark by DEA. The corresponding quenching rate constant k q of 0.15–0.3 × 109 M−1s−1 is below the diffusion controlled limit, namely that the electron acceptor behavior of NEC-CDots is somewhat unusual among its peers. A possible explanation for the obviously weaker electron accepting ability is due to modifications to the redox characteristics by the carbazole moieties on the dot surface, as similarly weak electron accepting properties were found in the PVK copolymers with carbon nanoparticles [27]. Mechanistic details on the possible modifications to redox characteristics remain to be explored in further investigations. Regardless of the mechanism(s), however, a significant implication of the results is that the photoinduced redox characteristics of CDots can be affected or modified through changes in surface passivation schemes.

The preparation of CDots by chemical functionalization of pre-processed and selected small carbon nanoparticles has a major advantage in terms of the unambiguous dot structures, simply because the core carbon nanoparticles are preexisting and also serve as template in the functionalization for CDots, without the structural complications often encountered in various carbonization syntheses from organic precursors. Most of the chemical functionalization reactions have targeted oxygen containing moieties like carboxylic acids on the nanoparticle surface, which are typically generated in the processing of the nanoparticles. Representing a new approach, the functionalization reported here targets directly surface carbons of the nanoparticles, with the resulting linkages much more robust and stable, as demonstrated by the insensitivity of the CDots to harsh conditions such as refluxing over an extended period of time. The approach may be used with a broad range of molecules that are capable of forming similar reactive species in the facile microwave processing for the functionalization of carbon nanoparticles, thus the ability to introduce a wide variety of functionalities into the resulting CDots.

Also relevant to introducing various desired functionalities into CDots are, in many cases, the needs for nonaqueous media in the functionalization and for solubility in or compatibility with common organic solvents, especially with respect to CDots designed for optoelectronics. The functionalization approach reported here is obviously compatible with such needs, with the NEC-CDots as a good example. These nonaqueous CDots share many attributes with similarly functionalized or derivatized fullerene cages, amenable to optoelectronic applications. The use of the same functionalization approach to introduce other optoelectronically significant moieties into CDots, such as those with thiophene derivatives for the functionalization, is justified and will be pursued in further investigations.

The apparently effective surface passivation in NEC-CDots for the observed high fluorescence quantum yields is rather striking. Structurally in the NEC-CDots, there are not many choices for possible configurations or arrangements between the carbon nanoparticle surface and the carbazole moieties. A more likely option is the proposed heteroaromatic carbazole pretty much lying flat on the surface (Fig. 1), thus the π stacking-like configuration and strong interactions. Such interactions, widely reported for other nanoscale carbon allotropes (carbon nanotubes and graphenes) but never for carbon nanoparticles, deserve further investigations, including for their serving as a unique mode of surface passivation for the core carbon nanoparticles in CDots.

A new approach in the functionalization of small carbon nanoparticles that targets directly the nanoparticle surface carbons can be implemented in a facile microwave-assisted processing. The NEC-CDots thus prepared are highly stable, exhibiting similar optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics to those found in CDots from other chemical functionalization syntheses. The observed bright fluorescence emissions of NEC-CDots are likely due to effective surface passivation of the core carbon nanoparticles by the heteroaromatic carbazole moieties lying flat on the nanoparticle surface via π stacking-like interactions. The new functionalization approach and the proposed new mode of surface passivation, both of major implications to the R&D of CDots with potentially broad applications, deserve further development and investigations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Air Force Office of Scientific Research through the program of Dr. Kenneth Caster, NASA/EPSCoR, Air Force Research Laboratory, and the South Carolina Space Grant Consortium is gratefully acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y.-P. Sun, Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, U.S. Patent 7829772, 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-Dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

Carbon dots: biomacromolecule interaction, bioimaging and nanomedicine
Carbon dots as photosensitisers for solar-driven catalysis
Synthesis, properties and biomedical applications of carbon-based quantum dots: an updated review
Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots

Modulation and effects of surface groups on photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity of carbon dots
ZnO/carbon quantum dots heterostructure with enhanced photocatalytic properties

Protein as the source for synthesizing fluorescent carbon dots by a one-pot hydrothermal route
